title: Pharmacy Calculations — NAPLEX
description: IV flow rates, dosing calculations, concentrations, compounding math, and clinical pharmacy calculations tested on the NAPLEX.
subject: pharmacy

cards.back: CrCl (mL/min) = [(140 − age) × weight(kg)] / [72 × SCr (mg/dL)]. Multiply by 0.85 for females. Use actual body weight (ABW), unless obese → use adjusted body weight (IBW + 0.4 × excess weight). Critical for renal dosing adjustments
Males: IBW = 50 kg + 2.3 kg × (inches over 5 ft). Females: IBW = 45.5 kg + 2.3 kg × (inches over 5 ft). Used for Cockcroft-Gault in obese patients and for aminoglycoside dosing

cards.front: IV flow rate: drops per minute (gtts/min)
cards.back: Gtts/min = (Volume (mL) × Drop factor (gtts/mL)) / Time (min). Standard macro drip: 10 or 15 gtts/mL. Micro drip: 60 gtts/mL. Example: 1000 mL over 8 hr with 20 gtts/mL = (1000 × 20) / 480 = 41.7 ≈ 42 gtts/min

cards.front: IV flow rate: mL/hr for infusion pump
cards.back: mL/hr = Total volume (mL) / Total time (hr). Example: 500 mL NS over 4 hr = 500/4 = 125 mL/hr

Example: Dopamine 5 mcg/kg/min for 70 kg patient from 400 mg/250 mL bag. Step 1: 5 mcg/kg/min × 70 kg = 350 mcg/min. Step 2: 350 mcg/min × 60 = 21,000 mcg/hr = 21 mg/hr. Step 3: concentration = 400/250 = 1.6 mg/mL. Rate = 21/1.6 = 13.1 mL/hr
Example: Heparin 25,000 units in 250 mL NS = 100 units/mL. Order: 1,200 units/hr. Rate = 1,200/100 = 12 mL/hr. Weight-based heparin: commonly 80 units/kg bolus, then 18 units/kg/hr
% w/v = g of solute per 100 mL of solution (most common). Example: 1% lidocaine = 1 g/100 mL = 10 mg/mL. % w/w = g per 100 g (creams, ointments). % v/v = mL per 100 mL (liquids)

mEq = (mg × valence) / molecular weight. Example: KCl 20 mEq — how many grams? MW of KCl = 74.5. grams = (mEq × MW) / (valence × 1000) = (20 × 74.5) / (1 × 1000) = 1.49 g. Important for electrolyte replacement calculations

Osmolarity (mOsm/L) = sum of (concentration × number of particles). Example: 0.9% NaCl: 154 mEq/L Na + 154 mEq/L Cl = 308 mOsm/L. D5W: 278 mOsm/L (5% dextrose). Serum osmolarity normal: 285–295 mOsm/L
C1 × V1 = C2 × V2. Example: Prepare 200 mL of 0.5% solution from 2% stock. V1 = (0.5 × 200) / 2 = 50 mL of 2% stock + 150 mL diluent. Always solve for the unknown variable

Used to find ratio of two solutions to achieve desired concentration. Parts of higher concentration / parts of lower concentration. Example: Mix 70% alcohol with 30% to make 50%. Higher: 50−30 = 20 parts; Lower: 70−50 = 20 parts. 1:1 ratio

PV = final volume (FV) − volume of diluent (VD) added. Example: Add 9.3 mL diluent to get 10 mL final volume → PV = 10 − 9.3 = 0.7 mL. Used when calculating exact drug concentration after reconstitution

cards.front: Days supply calculation for controlled substances
cards.back: Days supply = Quantity dispensed / (Dose × Frequency). Example: 120 tablets of hydrocodone/APAP 5/325, sig: 1 tab QID. Days = 120 / (1 × 4) = 30 days. Critical for controlled substance dispensing and insurance billing

cards.front: Pediatric dosing: Young's rule and Clark's rule
cards.back: Young's rule: Child dose = (Age(yr) / (Age + 12)) × Adult dose. Clark's rule: Child dose = (Weight(lb) / 150) × Adult dose. These are approximations; weight-based (mg/kg) dosing is preferred and more accurate in pediatric practice
BSA (m²) = √[(Height(cm) × Weight(kg)) / 3600]. Used for chemotherapy dosing, burn estimation, and pediatric dosing. Example: 70 kg, 170 cm: BSA = √(170 × 70 / 3600) = √3.306 = 1.82 m²

Traditional dosing: Peak (gentamicin/tobramycin): 5–10 mcg/mL (serious infections 6–10). Trough: < 2 mcg/mL (nephrotoxicity marker). Extended-interval (once daily): 15–30 mcg/mL peak acceptable; trough undetectable. Hartmann-Hartford nomogram used

Target AUC/MIC 400–600 (mg·h/L). AUC-based monitoring replaced trough-only monitoring to reduce nephrotoxicity while maintaining efficacy. AUC calculated from two-level (peak and trough) pharmacokinetic modeling. Target trough: 15–20 if AUC monitoring unavailable

Dextrose: 3.4 kcal/g. Lipids: 10 kcal/g (10%) or 20 kcal/g (20%). Amino acids: 4 kcal/g. GIR (glucose infusion rate) = mg/kg/min of dextrose. Maximum GIR: 4–5 mg/kg/min (avoid hyperglycemia). TPN kcal = dextrose kcal + lipid kcal + protein kcal

cards.front: Electrolyte content of common IV fluids (mEq/L)
cards.back: NS (0.9% NaCl): Na+ 154, Cl− 154. LR (lactated Ringer's): Na+ 130, K+ 4, Ca2+ 3, Cl− 109. D5W: no electrolytes (only dextrose). 0.45% NaCl (½ NS): Na+ 77, Cl− 77. D5LR: combines D5W + LR electrolytes

1–3 mcg/kg/min: dopaminergic receptors → ↑ renal/mesenteric blood flow. 3–10 mcg/kg/min: β1 receptors → ↑ HR, ↑ contractility. > 10 mcg/kg/min: α1 receptors → vasoconstriction, ↑ SVR. 'Renal dose dopamine' (1–3) is NOT evidence-based for renal protection

cards.front: Drug concentration from infusion: C = mg/mL
cards.back: Concentration (mg/mL) = Total mg added / Total volume (mL). Example: 2 g (2000 mg) of Drug X in 250 mL NS. C = 2000/250 = 8 mg/mL. Dose rate (mg/hr) / C = rate (mL/hr)

USP allowable variance: ±10% for non-sterile compounds (USP <795>), ±5% for sterile preparations (USP <797>). Potent drugs (e.g., warfarin, thyroid) often require ±5% or less. Calculate: % error = (actual − expected) / expected × 100

cards.front: Roman numerals used in prescription writing
cards.back: ss = ½, i = 1, ii = 2, iii = 3, iv = 4, v = 5, vi = 6, vii = 7, viii = 8, ix = 9, x = 10, xv = 15, xx = 20, xxx = 30. Old-style Rx writing; pharmacist must be able to interpret

Example: sliding scale order — for every 50 mg/dL above 150, give 1 unit regular insulin. Patient BG = 350. Calculation: (350 − 150) / 50 = 4 units. Understand unit-to-BG ratio and correct for patient-specific sliding scales

cards.front: Heparin units per mL and loading bolus
cards.back: Unfractionated heparin 1,000 units/mL is standard concentration. Loading dose example: 80 units/kg in 80 kg patient = 6,400 units. Draw up 6.4 mL from 1,000 units/mL vial. Maintenance: 18 units/kg/hr = 1,440 units/hr = 1.44 mL/hr (if 1,000 units/mL)

cards.front: Sodium bicarbonate: mEq calculation for metabolic acidosis
cards.back: Dose (mEq) = 0.5 × weight (kg) × (desired HCO3 − actual HCO3). Give half the dose first, then recheck ABG. Sodium bicarbonate 8.4% = 1 mEq/mL. 7.5% = 0.9 mEq/mL. Watch for sodium/volume overload and paradoxical CSF acidosis

For first 10 kg: 100 mL/kg/day (4 mL/kg/hr). Next 10 kg (10–20 kg): 50 mL/kg/day (2 mL/kg/hr). Each kg > 20 kg: 20 mL/kg/day (1 mL/kg/hr). Example: 25 kg child: (10×100) + (10×50) + (5×20) = 1,600 mL/day

REE males: 66.5 + (13.75 × kg) + (5 × cm) − (6.76 × age). REE females: 655 + (9.56 × kg) + (1.85 × cm) − (4.68 × age). Multiply by activity/stress factor. Critically ill: often 25–30 kcal/kg/day total

Corrected Ca2+ = Measured Ca2+ + 0.8 × (4 − measured albumin). Example: Measured Ca2+ = 7.2, albumin = 2.5. Corrected = 7.2 + 0.8 × (4 − 2.5) = 7.2 + 1.2 = 8.4 mg/dL. Critical because hypoalbuminemia masks hypocalcemia

AG = Na − (Cl + HCO3). Normal: 8–12 mEq/L (8–16 with K+). Elevated AG metabolic acidosis: MUDPILES (Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, INH/Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates). Delta-delta ratio to assess mixed disorders

cards.front: Acetaminophen maximum daily dose by population
cards.back: Healthy adult: 4 g/day (max single dose 1 g). Hepatic disease, alcohol use, elderly: 2–3 g/day. OTC products: 3.25 g/day per FDA. Hidden APAP in combo products: Norco (325 mg per tab × dosing frequency). Counseling: read ALL product labels

cards.front: INR supratherapeutic management: when to hold warfarin
cards.back: INR 4–10, no bleeding: hold 1–2 doses, recheck. INR > 10, no bleeding: hold + oral vitamin K 2.5–5 mg. INR any, serious bleeding: 4-factor PCC (Kcentra) + IV vitamin K 10 mg slow infusion. FFP if PCC unavailable. Restart warfarin when INR therapeutic

cards.front: Renal dosing for renally-cleared drugs: key thresholds
cards.back: Common renal adjustment thresholds: GFR < 50: gabapentin, metformin (use caution), digoxin. GFR < 30: metformin (contraindicated), most direct oral anticoagulants need adjustment. GFR < 15 (or dialysis): many antimicrobials need significant adjustment (ciprofloxacin, vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam)

Draw trough level 12 hours after last dose (for twice-daily dosing) or before morning dose. Steady state reached after 5 × t½ (~5 days). Therapeutic: 0.6–1.2 mEq/L (maintenance); 0.8–1.0 mEq/L (acute mania target)

cards.front: Warfarin dose adjustment based on INR
cards.back: INR 1.5–1.9 (sub-therapeutic): ↑ dose by 10–15%. INR 3.1–3.5: ↓ dose by 10%. INR 3.5–4.0: ↓ dose by 10–15%, check adherence. INR > 4.0: hold 1 dose and reduce. Document in notes; only adjust if pattern, not single measurement

QD/QAM = daily; BID = twice daily; TID = three times daily; QID = four times daily; QHS = at bedtime; PRN = as needed; AC = before meals; PC = after meals; NPO = nothing by mouth; SL = sublingual; IM = intramuscular; SQ/SC = subcutaneous; AU = both ears; OU = both eyes

eGFR (CKD-EPI or MDRD): standardized to 1.73 m² BSA — used for CKD staging. CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault): NOT BSA-adjusted — used for drug dosing calculations. For drug dosing: always use Cockcroft-Gault. Common board-tested distinction

cards.front: Phenytoin dosing: why Vd-based loading is important
cards.back: Phenytoin LD = 15–20 mg/kg IV (1 mg/kg gives ~1 mcg/mL). Obese patients: use adjusted body weight (IBW + 1.33 × excess). Maintenance: 5–7 mg/kg/day (renally adjusted not needed). Levels: total 10–20 mcg/mL; correct for low albumin or renal failure

Corrected level = measured level / (0.2 × albumin + 0.1). For renal failure (with or without low albumin): measured / (0.1 × albumin + 0.1). Use corrected level to determine true efficacy; avoid toxicity when true level is higher than measured

Loading dose: 10–15 mcg/kg (lean body weight) divided over 24 hr (e.g., 0.5 mg + 0.25 mg q6h). Onset: 30 min PO, 5–30 min IV. Therapeutic range: 0.5–2 ng/mL (atrial fibrillation: 0.5–1.0 preferred). Draw trough level ≥ 6–8 hrs after last dose

Measured osmolality − Calculated osmolality = Osmolal gap. Calculated = 2(Na) + (BUN/2.8) + (glucose/18). Normal gap: < 10 mOsm/kg. Elevated gap: toxic alcohols (methanol, ethylene glycol, isopropanol). Used to detect unmeasured osmoles

cards.front: QTc prolongation: Bazett's formula and threshold
cards.back: QTc = QT / √(RR interval in seconds). Normal QTc: < 440 ms (men), < 460 ms (women). Risk of torsades: > 500 ms. Pharmacist responsibility: screen all QT-prolonging drug combinations. Baseline ECG warranted before starting high-risk drugs

NTG for unstable angina/hypertensive urgency: start 5–10 mcg/min, titrate by 5–10 mcg/min q3–5 min. Concentration: 100 mcg/mL (100 mg/250 mL is 400 mcg/mL for fluid-restricted). Calculate mL/hr = mcg/min × 60 / concentration (mcg/mL)

cards.front: Antibiotic stewardship: PK/PD targets for common drug classes
cards.back: Time-dependent (maximize %T > MIC): β-lactams — extend infusion or increase frequency. Concentration-dependent (maximize Cmax/MIC): aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones — high dose once daily. AUC/MIC: vancomycin. Apply to optimize dosing and reduce resistance

1 mg = pink, 2 mg = lavender, 2.5 mg = green, 3 mg = tan, 4 mg = blue, 5 mg = peach, 6 mg = teal, 7.5 mg = yellow, 10 mg = white. Color coding helps detect dispensing errors. Counsel patients on their specific tablet color and dose

Isotonic: NaCl 0.9% (308 mOsm/L), LR, D5W (278 mOsm/L). Hypotonic: < 280 mOsm/L (0.45% NaCl). Hypertonic: > 320 mOsm/L (D10W, 3% NaCl, mannitol 20%). Hypertonic solutions: administer via central line to prevent phlebitis

Mild dehydration (5%): 50 mL/kg deficit. Moderate (10%): 100 mL/kg. Severe (15%): 150 mL/kg. Replacement: give ½ deficit in first 8 hr + maintenance; remainder over next 16 hr. Use isotonic fluid (LR or NS) in most cases

Carboplatin dose (mg) = Target AUC (mg·mL/min) × (GFR + 25). Target AUC: 5–7 for previously untreated, 4–5 for retreatment. Uses measured/estimated GFR. FDA recommended maximum dose cap: use capped GFR for obese patients to prevent overdosing

cards.front: Bioequivalence criteria for generic drug approval (FDA)
cards.back: 80–125% rule: generic AUC and Cmax must fall within 80–125% of the reference product's 90% confidence interval. This does NOT mean ±20% — the confidence interval must fit within that range (stricter). Narrow TI drugs (NTI): tighter acceptance criteria (90–111.11%)

pH = pKa + log([A−]/[HA]). Determines ionization state of drugs at physiologic pH. Weak acids (aspirin, barbiturates): ionized in alkaline urine → renal excretion ↑ with alkalinization (useful in overdose treatment). Weak bases: ionized in acidic urine → trapped in urine
